(06/03/26)

Broadcom delivered a fundamentally powerful Q2 FY26 print, but the call’s investment significance was more nuanced than the headline growth rates. Reported Q2 revenue was $22.187B, up 48% YoY and 15% QoQ, modestly above prior company guidance of approximately $22.0B. Non-GAAP operating income was $14.928B, implying a 67.3% operating margin, while adjusted EBITDA was $15.244B, or approximately 69% of revenue, above the prior 68% guide. Free cash flow was $10.262B, or 46% of revenue, up 60% YoY. The quarter therefore validated the core operating-leverage thesis: Broadcom is scaling AI semiconductor revenue at extreme velocity while preserving software-like consolidated operating margins and exceptional cash conversion. Official results confirm Q2 revenue of $22.187B, adjusted EBITDA of $15.244B, non-GAAP EPS of $2.44, and free cash flow of $10.262B.  

The equity-market reaction was not driven by deteriorating fundamentals; it was driven by expectation risk. External reports were mixed on whether the quarter modestly beat or missed consensus, depending on the consensus source, but Reuters reported Q2 revenue of $22.19B versus LSEG expectations of $22.27B and Q3 AI semiconductor guidance of $16.0B versus Visible Alpha expectations of $16.36B. This is the critical setup issue: Broadcom delivered a beat versus its own guide, raised the forward revenue trajectory sharply, and reiterated the FY27 AI semiconductor revenue target of more than $100B, yet the magnitude of upside did not clear the more aggressive buyside hurdle embedded in the stock after a substantial AI-driven move.  

The most important call message was management’s explicit escalation of AI visibility. Hock Tan stated that “Demand for XPUs and networking is simply insatiable” and that “During the quarter, bookings for AI semiconductors were over $30 billion against the $10.8 billion we shipped.” That implies an AI semiconductor book-to-bill ratio of more than 2.8x in Q2, which is extraordinary for a semiconductor business of this scale. The call also moved the visibility window materially outward: management stated that visibility now runs to 2028, compared with visibility largely through 2027 only 3 months ago. That extension is highly investment-relevant because the AI ramp is no longer framed as a 2026 demand spike; it is being framed as a multi-year capacity deployment cycle across 6 core customers.

Q2 AI semiconductor revenue was $10.8B, up 143% YoY and 29% QoQ, modestly above the Q1 call guide of $10.7B. AI represented 49% of total company revenue and approximately 72% of Semiconductor Solutions revenue. Networking represented almost 40% of Q2 AI revenue, implying approximately $4.3B of AI networking revenue if the 40% reference is treated as approximate, versus approximately $2.8B in Q1 if the Q1 33% mix reference is applied to $8.4B of Q1 AI revenue. This suggests AI networking revenue may have grown more than 50% QoQ, faster than total AI revenue, and remains central to the quality of the growth. Q1 transcript data show prior Q2 guidance of $14.8B in semiconductor revenue, $10.7B in AI semiconductor revenue, $7.2B in infrastructure software revenue, and approximately 68% adjusted EBITDA margin.  

The Q2 beat versus prior guidance was real but not dramatic. Total revenue exceeded guide by $187M, or 0.9%; semiconductor revenue exceeded the $14.8B prior segment guide by approximately $209M, or 1.4%; AI semiconductor revenue exceeded the $10.7B guide by $100M, or 0.9%; infrastructure software was essentially in line at $7.178B versus the prior $7.2B guide. Adjusted EBITDA was the cleaner upside element, beating the dollar implied by prior revenue and margin guidance by approximately $284M. The quarter therefore should be interpreted as a high-quality execution quarter with modest revenue upside and stronger operating leverage, not as a material near-term demand surprise.

The Q3 guide is the true centerpiece of the call. Management guided Q3 FY26 revenue to $29.4B, up 84% YoY and 33% QoQ. Semiconductor revenue is expected to be $20.5B, up 124% YoY and 37% QoQ. AI semiconductor revenue is expected to be $16.0B, up more than 200% YoY and 48% QoQ. Non-AI semiconductor revenue is expected to be approximately $4.5B, up 12% YoY and 7% QoQ. Infrastructure software revenue is expected to be $8.9B, up 31% YoY and 24% QoQ. The official Q2 release confirms Q3 revenue guidance of approximately $29.4B, non-GAAP operating income guidance of approximately 67% of projected revenue, and adjusted EBITDA guidance of approximately 68% of projected revenue.  

Relative to Q3 FY25, the guided acceleration is extreme. Broadcom’s Q3 FY25 revenue was $15.952B, with Semiconductor Solutions revenue of $9.166B, infrastructure software revenue of $6.786B, and AI revenue of $5.2B. The Q3 FY26 guide therefore implies total revenue growth of 84%, semiconductor growth of 124%, software growth of 31%, and AI semiconductor growth of roughly 208%. That degree of acceleration from an already scaled revenue base is rare, even within AI infrastructure.  

The FY26 AI semiconductor revenue guide of $56B is highly consequential. Broadcom generated $8.4B of AI revenue in Q1 and $10.8B in Q2, for $19.2B in 1H26. With Q3 guided at $16.0B, the $56B FY26 guide implies Q4 AI semiconductor revenue of approximately $20.8B. That would represent 30% sequential growth in Q4 and 2H26 AI revenue of $36.8B, or 1.9x 1H26. The Q&A discussion included confusion around the 2H26 versus 1H26 math, but the key conclusion is straightforward: the $56B FY26 AI target does not imply a Q4 decline; it implies a very large Q4 step-up after an already large Q3 step-up.

The FY27 reiteration matters more than the Q3 guide. Management reiterated that FY27 AI semiconductor revenue is expected to be “in excess of $100 billion.” On a base of $56B in FY26, the lower bound of that statement implies at least 79% YoY growth in FY27. Relative to approximately $20B of FY25 AI revenue, a $100B FY27 level implies a 2-year CAGR of approximately 124%. Hock Tan explicitly said that 2027 is “very much on-track, if not stronger,” while also declining to provide a quarter-by-quarter FY27 guide. That language preserves optionality for upside without formally underwriting the $200B 18-month backlog figure raised by an analyst in Q&A.

Management did not validate the analyst’s $200B AI backlog framing. This distinction is important. The call disclosed more than $30B of Q2 AI semiconductor bookings and reiterated a more than $100B FY27 AI semiconductor revenue outlook, but management did not explicitly endorse the suggestion that the next 18-month AI backlog is $200B or higher. The appropriate interpretation is that order intake, customer commitments, and gigawatt roadmaps are moving materially ahead of prior expectations, but precise backlog extrapolations beyond disclosed bookings and guideposts remain speculative. The call strengthened the visibility thesis; it did not convert every long-dated capacity indication into firm backlog.

The customer roadmap was materially de-risking, but also highlighted concentration. Google remains the anchor customer, and the April long-term agreement is the most important strategic confirmation. Broadcom’s Form 8-K states that Broadcom and Google entered into a long-term agreement for Broadcom to develop and supply custom TPUs for future generations and a supply assurance agreement for networking and other components used in Google’s next-generation AI racks through up to 2031.   On the call, management emphasized that the relationship remains “strategic and very substantial,” while also acknowledging that Google will maintain some source diversity given the scale of its AI compute requirements. That admission is analytically important: the agreement meaningfully de-risks roadmap participation, but it does not eliminate share-of-wallet risk.

Anthropic is emerging as a material incremental demand vector. The supplied transcript states that Broadcom is providing access to more than 1GW of TPU-based compute in 2026 and that an April agreement enables Anthropic to access another 5GW of next-generation TPU-based compute beginning in 2027. The April 8-K disclosed approximately 3.5GW accessed through Broadcom beginning in 2027 as part of Anthropic’s multiple-gigawatt next-generation TPU-based capacity commitment, while Anthropic separately stated that the new agreement with Google and Broadcom covers multiple gigawatts of next-generation TPU capacity expected to come online starting in 2027. The difference between the transcript’s 5GW reference and the 8-K’s approximately 3.5GW “through Broadcom” language likely reflects scope differences, and models should treat the 8-K as the tighter legal disclosure.    

OpenAI is a major late-2026 and FY27 ramp variable. Broadcom’s prior OpenAI announcement disclosed a collaboration for 10GW of custom AI accelerators, with Broadcom to deploy racks of accelerator and network systems targeted to start in 2H26 and complete by year-end 2029. The Q2 call added that silicon has been delivered, production remains on track for late 2026, and there is a contractual commitment to deploy 1.3GW in 2027 as part of the larger 10GW agreement. This is a critical bridge from design-win narrative to revenue recognition. It also increases dependence on OpenAI’s ability to finance, power, and absorb very large custom accelerator deployments.  

Meta is a stronger long-term contributor than near-term contributor. The April Meta partnership confirmed a multi-year, multi-generation strategic partnership around MTIA custom silicon, advanced packaging, and Ethernet networking, with an initial commitment exceeding 1GW and a sustained multi-gigawatt rollout. The Q2 call added that Broadcom expects to deploy 3GW through the end of 2028 and has received the initial 1GW order, with deliveries starting in 2H27. This supports FY28 growth visibility more than FY26 upside. Meta also validates Broadcom’s positioning beyond the Google TPU ecosystem and supports the broader thesis that hyperscalers increasingly want workload-specific accelerators rather than only merchant GPUs.  

The other 2 customers remain less transparent but are no longer immaterial. Management said shipments for the other 2 customers are expected to begin late 2026 and accelerate into 2027, with purchase orders totaling $6B received to date. That disclosure is important because it implies customer diversification is beginning to move from pipeline to purchase orders. However, these customers remain less de-risked than Google, Anthropic, OpenAI, and Meta due to limited program-level detail, limited public customer identification, and unclear deployment cadence.

The technology narrative remains centered on Broadcom’s combination of custom XPU execution and AI networking leadership. Management’s most important strategic claim was not simply that Broadcom can build custom accelerators; it was that custom accelerators require high-performance cluster networking, and Broadcom is advantaged across scale-up, scale-out, scale-across, Ethernet switching, SerDes, DSPs, lasers, PCIe switching, and Jericho fabrics. Hock Tan stated that in networking Broadcom has “at least one generation of technology and product leadership.” He cited direct-attached copper based on 200G and 400G SerDes, the industry’s only 100Tb Ethernet switch shipping for more than 1 year, a next-generation 200Tb switch taping out this quarter, 1.6Tb DSPs, CW and EML lasers, and Jericho 3 and Jericho 4 fabric solutions.

The networking mix has 2-sided margin and growth implications. In Q2, networking represented almost 40% of AI revenue, but management suggested the more normalized share of total AI revenue may be closer to 30% as XPU shipments scale. This matters because AI networking carries “very rich margins,” while ASICs and TPUs carry lower margins. A decline in networking mix could pressure semiconductor gross margin, all else equal, but the offset is that XPU volume is the primary driver of absolute AI revenue dollars. The investment implication is that gross margin should not be modeled as a simple function of AI revenue growth; it is a function of software mix, networking mix, XPU mix, and generational content per gigawatt.

The call clarified that Broadcom remains a chip/content supplier rather than a rack integrator. When asked about rack versus chip economics, management stated bluntly, “No racks is all chips” and “We only chips.” This is a key point for valuation and margin modeling. It reduces concern that Broadcom is moving into lower-margin rack integration, but it also means revenue per gigawatt should be modeled as Broadcom silicon and connectivity content rather than full infrastructure bill of materials. The distinction is particularly important when comparing Broadcom’s implied AI opportunity against Nvidia, ODM, memory, power, and data-center infrastructure revenue pools.

Margin quality was one of the strongest aspects of the call. Consolidated gross margin was 77.1% in Q2, down 230bps YoY as semiconductors became a larger proportion of mix, but operating margin rose 200bps YoY to 67.3% because operating expenses remained relatively flat. Semiconductor gross margin was approximately 70%, and semiconductor operating margin rose to 62%, up 460bps YoY. Infrastructure software gross margin was 93%, and software operating margin was approximately 79%, up 310bps YoY. The combination shows that AI mix dilution at gross margin is being more than offset below the gross profit line through scale. The official Q2 release reports Semiconductor Solutions revenue of $15.009B, infrastructure software revenue of $7.178B, and non-GAAP operating income of $14.928B.  

Q3 margin guidance is the key model stress test. Management guided consolidated gross margin down to approximately 74%, a roughly 310bps sequential decline from Q2, due to the significantly higher proportion of AI semiconductor revenue. However, non-GAAP operating margin is expected to remain approximately 67%, essentially flat sequentially, and adjusted EBITDA is expected to remain approximately 68% of revenue. Kirsten Spears emphasized that “This decline in gross margin does not represent a structural change in semiconductor margin. Rather, it reflects product mix between semiconductors and infrastructure software.” The correct investor focus is therefore operating income dollars, EBITDA dollars, and free cash flow, not gross margin in isolation.

The free cash flow profile remains exceptional and strategically important. Q2 free cash flow of $10.3B represented 46% of revenue, while capex was only $231M. Cash increased to $19.6B from $14.2B in Q1. This cash generation provides strategic flexibility to fund R&D, support supply-chain commitments, maintain dividends, and potentially repurchase shares, although Q2 capital return in the transcript was limited to the $3.1B dividend payment. Broadcom’s model remains structurally capital-light relative to the scale of AI infrastructure it enables, which is a major reason the equity can command a premium multiple if the AI revenue path remains credible.  

Inventory is a deliberate supply-chain signal, not a simple working-capital negative. Inventory increased to $4.3B, and days of inventory on hand rose to 86 days from 68 days in Q1, specifically to secure supply ahead of accelerating AI semiconductor growth in 2H26. Given the Q3 AI revenue guide of $16B and implied Q4 AI revenue of approximately $20.8B, the inventory build appears consistent with customer demand rather than channel stuffing. The risk is that a deployment delay at a large customer could leave Broadcom carrying high-value AI inventory, but current bookings and guide cadence reduce that concern.

Infrastructure software was better than the headline Q2 growth rate suggests. Q2 infrastructure software revenue grew 9% YoY to $7.2B and was in line with guidance, while ARR grew 17% YoY. More importantly, Q3 software revenue is guided to $8.9B, up 31% YoY and 24% QoQ. Management tied the acceleration to VMware Cloud Foundation 9.1, strong global server demand, on-prem cloud computing deployments, and support for enterprise AI inferencing workloads across AMD, Intel, and Nvidia CPU/GPU platforms. The strategic implication is that AI is not just a semiconductor accelerator for Broadcom; it may also be increasing the relevance of VMware as a private-cloud operating layer for heterogeneous compute.

The software segment also remains a key valuation stabilizer. Software gross margin of 93% and operating margin of 79% provide a high-margin cash engine that helps absorb semiconductor cyclicality and AI supply-chain investment. The potential concern is mix perception: as AI semiconductors grow faster, software will become a smaller percentage of total revenue even if software dollars continue to rise. Investors may increasingly value Broadcom as an AI semiconductor platform rather than as a balanced semiconductor/software compounder, which can increase multiple volatility.

Non-AI semiconductors are improving but no longer define the thesis. Q2 non-AI semiconductor revenue was $4.2B, up 6% YoY, and bookings exceeded $6B, implying a book-to-bill ratio above 1.4x. Management described this as evidence that Broadcom is on the path toward a full cyclical recovery. Q3 non-AI semiconductor revenue is guided to approximately $4.5B, up 12% YoY. Broadband, server storage, and enterprise networking improved, partially offset by wireless seasonality. This recovery is helpful because it reduces drag and broadens growth, but it is secondary to the AI semiconductor trajectory.

The AI XPV platform with Apollo, Blackstone, and other investors is strategically powerful but introduces a new analytical layer. Management described a platform intended to deploy more than 20GW of compute capacity through 2028, with the 1st tranche valued at $35B and currently being launched by Apollo. The strategic logic is clear: frontier labs such as Anthropic and OpenAI require massive compute capacity but may benefit from third-party capital partners with stronger balance sheets. For Broadcom, this could pull forward silicon demand, improve order visibility, and reduce friction for customers. The risk is that Broadcom’s revenue trajectory becomes increasingly tied to external project finance, power availability, site readiness, and LLM customer monetization.

The call also reinforced that AI demand is moving from hyperscaler internal workloads to frontier-model token consumption, but not necessarily to direct enterprise chip purchases. In response to a question on enterprise AI demand, Hock Tan stated that enterprise consumption is still “relatively at an early stage,” and that enterprises are consuming tokens through platforms such as Anthropic, OpenAI, and Gemini rather than buying XPUs directly. This has 2 implications. 1st, Broadcom’s real demand source is concentrated in a small number of frontier model developers and hyperscalers. 2nd, if enterprise token demand accelerates, the revenue benefit still flows through the same small set of Broadcom customers, amplifying concentration but also increasing scalability.

Supply availability was addressed constructively. Hock Tan stated that Broadcom is “very comfortable” it has secured supply for 2026 and 2027 and is working on 2028 and 2029. He also said customers have been asking for incremental supply over recent months and that, “by and large,” Broadcom has been able to support it. This is an important rebuttal to concerns that wafers, HBM, substrates, or advanced packaging could cap upside. However, the answer did not eliminate supply-chain risk; it reframed it as manageable under current planning assumptions.

The biggest positive investment implication is that Broadcom’s AI revenue path now has unusually strong multi-year visibility for a semiconductor business. The combination of $30B-plus Q2 AI bookings, a $56B FY26 AI guide, more than $100B FY27 AI guidance, 10GW planned shipments in FY27, and visible customer-specific gigawatt ramps supports the view that Broadcom is becoming one of the 2 most strategically important AI silicon suppliers globally, alongside Nvidia but with a differentiated custom XPU and Ethernet networking model. The call did not make Broadcom less cyclical; it made the cycle longer, larger, and more customer-roadmap-driven.

The biggest negative investment implication is that expectations have become extremely difficult to beat. A Q3 revenue guide 84% above the prior year and above sell-side consensus still produced a negative after-hours stock reaction because the AI guide did not exceed the most aggressive expectations. This is the definition of a crowded long with a high bar. The stock may now require evidence that FY27 AI revenue is not merely above $100B but materially above $100B, or that FY28 gigawatt deployments are accelerating faster than modeled. The call was fundamentally constructive but did not eliminate near-term multiple compression risk.

The most important modeling debate is FY27 AI revenue. If FY26 AI revenue is $56B and FY27 exceeds $100B, then the minimum incremental AI revenue is $44B. However, management’s comments on 10GW of FY27 shipments and rising content per gigawatt suggest that the true number could be meaningfully higher if deployments execute on time. At the same time, management explicitly avoided formalizing a number beyond “in excess of $100B.” The prudent modeling approach is to treat $100B as a floor case contingent on execution, not as a midpoint, while stress-testing slippage in OpenAI, Anthropic, Meta, and the 2 less-disclosed customers.

The 2nd most important modeling debate is gross margin versus operating margin. Q3 gross margin guidance falls to 74%, but operating margin remains at 67%. This is not a contradiction; it reflects very high operating leverage and the mix shift from software to semiconductors. The stock should be evaluated on operating income and free cash flow durability, not gross margin optics alone. Still, if AI networking mix normalizes from almost 40% toward 30% while XPU mix rises, semiconductor gross margin could face pressure even if total operating income continues to expand.

The 3rd most important debate is competitive durability. Broadcom has a compelling position in custom silicon, SerDes, Ethernet switching, DSPs, lasers, and PCIe connectivity. However, competition remains intense across Nvidia’s GPU ecosystem, Marvell’s custom silicon efforts, internal hyperscaler chip teams, alternative networking approaches, and customer-owned tooling. Reuters noted intensifying competition from Nvidia and Marvell in custom semiconductors, and the broader AI infrastructure market remains highly dynamic.   The call reduced near-term competitive concern through customer agreement disclosures, but it did not remove long-term share risk.

The 4th debate is AI infrastructure financing and customer ROI. The XPV platform is a creative mechanism to unlock compute deployments for frontier labs, but it also confirms that AI compute demand is becoming so capital-intensive that balance-sheet engineering is part of the growth model. This is positive if token demand and model monetization scale as expected. It is negative if financing costs, power constraints, regulatory pressure, or customer revenue realization cause deployment schedules to slip. The market will likely scrutinize any signal that OpenAI, Anthropic, or other frontier labs are slowing compute absorption.

The bottom-line investment read-through is that the Q2 call strengthened the structural long-term Broadcom thesis while worsening the near-term setup by confirming that consensus expectations have moved aggressively ahead. The fundamental data points were overwhelmingly positive: record revenue, record operating income, record EBITDA, record free cash flow, AI bookings far above shipments, FY26 AI revenue guided to $56B, FY27 AI revenue reiterated above $100B, 2028 visibility expanding, customer roadmaps broadening, and software growth reaccelerating. The offset is that valuation now depends on flawless execution across customer ramps, supply commitments, power infrastructure, AI financing structures, and margin mix. The call was not a thesis-breaker; it was a bar-raiser.

CALL PARTICIPANTS - RESEARCH ANALYSTS

• Harlan Sur, JPMorgan

• Blayne Curtis, Jefferies

• Ross Seymore, Deutsche Bank

• Ben Reitzes, Melius Research

• Timothy Arcuri, UBS

• Stacy Rasgon, Bernstein Research

• Jim Schneider, Goldman Sachs

• Tom O’Malley, Barclays

• CJ Muse, Cantor Fitzgerald

• Atif Malik, Citi

• Edward Snyder, Charter Equity Research

• Joseph Moore, Morgan Stanley

text:
Bear with me but I dug deep into this. 

Credo is sampling the Cardinal DSP family to lead customers. 

1.6T transceiver was in demonstration-  stage at OFC but based on bluebird dsp

Bluebird was first gen dsp while this new cardinal dsp is second gen and was engineered with integrated EML + silicon photonics drivers, which is what ties it to the DustPhotonics acquisition.

Owning the DSP, the silicon photonics, and the transceiver assembly is vertical integration

optical transceiver manufacturers buy DSP silicon and build it into modules. The major names: Fabrinet, Coherent, Lumentum, Innolight, Eoptolink, plus others. DSP is a key component inside. 

But when Credo sells its own ZeroFlap transceiver, it competes with these module makers instead — the vertical-integration angle from earlier.)

The end demand. Google and Amazon are qualifying 1.6T ￼, with the broader set being Microsoft, Meta, Oracle, and the neoclouds. They don’t usually buy the DSP directly, but they run the qualification that determines which DSP wins. 

However Once a module maker qualifies a Marvell or Broadcom DSP into a platform that a hyperscaler has blessed, switching to Credo means re-qualifying and that’s not as simple. 

So that’s where the vertically integrated nature is a game changer. 

By owning the DSP (Cardinal/Bluebird), the silicon photonics/laser engine (DustPhotonics), and the transceiver assembly with reliability telemetry (ZeroFlap/PILOT), 
$CRDO controls the three highest-value blocks of the 1.6T module in-house — capturing more BOM dollars per link, tuning the electrical-optical-DSP interfaces together for a lower-power and more reliable module than rivals stitching together third-party components, and sidestepping the merchant-DSP socket fight by selling a differentiated whole module rather than a chip that has to displace entrenched Marvell/Broadcom silicon.

Their is why their guidance increase was highly encouraging. They are winning in optical and likely entire modules 3 digit ASPs.

text:
People think the AI infrastructure / photonics / semiconductor trade is over because some stocks already ran.

Think again.

The market is still massively underestimating what Europe is about to do through the EU Chips Act and now Chips Act 2.0.

The EU is actively pushing to accelerate domestic semiconductor demand, AI infrastructure, and photonic chip development.

Years from now, this decade may not be remembered for apps or software…

but for the Physical AI infrastructure layer that quietly rebuilt the world underneath it.

$AMBA $SIVE $XFAB $ARBE $AEVA $OUST $LAES

text:
China is blessed: 
Silicon Photonics and Chip-level Optical I/O. At a major IEEE conference, Huawei formally introduced 'Tao's Law' (\tau-Law), which highlights their massive breakthrough in replacing traditional copper wires with lasers and fiber optics right at the chip level

$IQE.L 업데이트

IQE가 6월 3일 RNS를 통해 Artisan Partners 계열의 major holding 변동을 공시함.

핵심은 Artisan Partners 측 IQE 의결권 지분이 기존 10.60%에서 13.10%로 올라갔다는 점임.

공시 기준으로 Artisan 측은 IQE 보통주 의결권 171,991,595개를 보유하고 있고, 이는 전체 의결권의 13.10%임.

금융상품을 통한 추가 의결권은 0%.

기준일은 2026년 6월 1일, 회사 통보일은 6월 3일임.

다만 이걸 “Artisan이 장내에서 대규모로 추가 매수했다”고 바로 단정하면 안 됨.

이번 TR-1에는 voting rights 취득/처분뿐 아니라, 총 의결권 구조 변화도 같이 반영된 것으로 보임.

IQE는 최근 fundraising 완료 이후 발행주식수와 총 의결권 기준이 바뀌었음.

그래서 실제 추가 매수인지, 배정 참여인지, 의결권 기준 변화 영향인지까지는 추가 확인이 필요함.

그래도 긍정적으로 볼 부분은 있음.

최근 IQE는 £81M 자금조달을 마쳤고, MACOM 전략투자와 장기공급계약도 들어왔음.

여기에 Artisan 같은 기관 주주가 10%대에서 13%대로 올라온 공시가 나오면서, IQE의 주주구성과 자본구조 재편이 본격적으로 드러나고 있음.

물론 이건 신규 고객계약도 아니고, InP / AI datacenter photonics 매출 전환 확인도 아님.

이번 Artisan 지분 공시는 강한 펀더멘털 호재라기보다는 hold-strengthening 보조 신호에 가까움.

단순 테마성 반등이 아니라, 전략투자자와 기관 주주가 동시에 의미 있는 지분을 보유하는 구조로 바뀌고 있다는 점은 긍정적임.

다만 dilution 이후 주주구성 재편 과정이기 때문에 과도한 해석은 금물임.

다음 체크포인트는 세 가지임.

Artisan 지분 증가의 정확한 원인
MACOM 측 이사 선임 완료 여부
MACOM LTSA와 AI photonics 수요가 실제 revenue / EBITDA recovery로 연결되는지

개인 기록 / 투자 조언 아님.

IQE는 최근 fundraising 완료 이후 발행주식수와 총 의결권 기준이 바뀌었음. 그래서 실제 추가 매수인지, 배정 참여인지, 의결권 기준 변화 영향인지까지는 추가 확인이 필요함. 그래도 긍정적으로 볼 부분은 있음. 최근 IQE는 £81M 자금조달을 마쳤고, MACOM 전략투자와 장기공급계약도 들어왔음. 여기에 Artisan 같은 기관 주주가 10%대에서 13%대로 올라온 공시가 나오면서, IQE의 주주구성과 자본구조 재편이 본격적으로 드러나고 있음. 물론 이건 신규 고객계약도 아니고, InP / AI datacenter photonics 매출 전환 확인도 아님. 이번 Artisan 지분 공시는 강한 펀더멘털 호재라기보다는 hold-strengthening 보조 신호에 가까움. 단순 테마성 반등이 아니라, 전략투자자와 기관 주주가 동시에 의미 있는 지분을 보유하는 구조로 바뀌고 있다는 점은 긍정적임. 다만 dilution 이후 주주구성 재편 과정이기 때문에 과도한 해석은 금물임. 다음 체크포인트는 세 가지임. Artisan 지분 증가의 정확한 원인 MACOM 측 이사 선임 완료 여부 MACOM LTSA와 AI photonics 수요가 실제 revenue / EBITDA recovery로 연결되는지 개인 기록 / 투자 조언 아님."}}}, "rest_id": "2062310958656266737", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "976", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
Since $MRVL was up +32%, let me introduce you Taiwan’s Most Direct $MRVL related Stock: LuxNet Corporation 華星光（4979):

Marvell’s push into high-speed Optical DSPs and silicon photonics/CPO solutions for AI data centers has created clear demand for supporting photonic components. LuxNet stands out because of its specific role and long-term relationship with Marvell.

Here’s why the linkage is considered particularly direct:

1.  Critical Photonic Component Supplier
 LuxNet produces InP-based CW Lasers, which serve as the essential external light source in silicon photonics and CPO architectures. These lasers are a key part of enabling Marvell’s advanced Optical DSP platforms.

2.  Long-Standing Partnership The relationship goes back to Marvell’s acquisition of Inphi. 

LuxNet has been a manufacturing and development partner for Marvell’s high-speed optical transceiver modules and continues to work with the company on next-generation solutions.

3.  Frequently Cited in Market Coverage
 When Marvell reports progress in optical or silicon photonics, or when Nvidia deepens cooperation with Marvell, LuxNet is regularly included in analyst and media lists of the most direct Taiwan beneficiaries.
 Example: Recent coverage on Nvidia-Marvell silicon photonics collaboration highlighted LuxNet as part of the Taiwan optical supply chain that stands to benefit.
4.  Higher Specificity Than Most Peers Unlike broader semiconductor or foundry plays, LuxNet’s involvement is more component-specific and tied directly to Marvell’s optical product roadmap, making the supply chain connection more visible and targeted.

This combination of technical role, historical partnership, and consistent market recognition is why LuxNet is often viewed as one of the clearest $MRVL supply chain exposures in Taiwan.

#MRVL #LuxNet #4979 #CPO #SiliconPhotonics

text:
Wind Financial Morning Post: June 3, 2026

Market Brief

The State Council issues the "15th Five-Year Plan for Accelerating Agricultural and Rural Modernization"

China achieves significant breakthrough in optical communication technology

Shanghai establishes a new landmark for the global asset management center: scale to reach RMB 5.5 trillion by 2030

Prospects for U.S.-Iran negotiations remain uncertain, while applications for transit permits through the Strait of Hormuz have opened

The United States lowers import tariffs on selected steel, aluminum, and copper products, and plans to impose a new 25% tariff on Brazil

Global stock markets rose Tuesday, with U.S. markets hitting new highs and South Korea’s market entering the world’s top six largest securities markets

Tiger Brokers: Suspends new position opening and additional positioning for existing investor accounts in all domestic Chinese instruments effective June 12

Microsoft releases seven new AI models to create an integrated “thinking + programming” agent ecosystem

Top Stories

Shanghai has unveiled 21 measures to deepen its construction as a global asset management center, aiming for total asset management scale of 5.4 trillion yuan by 2030, accounting for one-third of the national total. The city will support listings and mergers of technology firms with strong innovation profiles; accelerate the launch of LNG futures and options; prepare for power and computing power futures; promote the listing of Star 50 Index, Shenzhen 100 Index, ChiNext Index futures and options, and government bond options; enhance mechanisms such as “Shanghai-Hong Kong Stock Connect,” “China-Europe Connect,” and “Bond Connect”; and steadily expand institutional openness in the gold market.

Tiger Brokers announced that, to comply with regulatory requirements during the cross-border securities business consolidation period, it will adjust services for existing investor accounts in mainland China starting June 12. Measures include suspending new positions and additional purchases across all securities products, permitting only sell and close-out transactions; and halting inbound fund transfers while maintaining outbound transfer functionality. The company reported first-quarter revenue of $155 million, up 26.3% year-over-year, and operating profit of $48 million, up 17.5%.

According to Iranian sources, the system for applying for vessel transit permits through the Strait of Hormuz has been fully opened, allowing global shipowners and captains to submit applications. Upon approval, vessels will receive transit authorization. Iranian media reported that information exchanges between Iran and the U.S. have been suspended for several days, despite Western media and officials claiming ongoing dialogue. U.S. President Trump stated that talks with Iran continue. U.S. Secretary of State Rubio affirmed that negotiations are still underway but expressed uncertainty over a potential agreement.

Microsoft unveiled seven new AI models at the Build 2026 developer conference, covering full-stack capabilities in reasoning, coding, vision, speech, and multimodal functions. Its flagship reasoning model, MAI-Thinking-1, matched Anthropic’s Claude Sonnet 4.6 on coding benchmarks. The 5-billion-parameter coding model, MAI-Code-1-Flash, targets the GitHub ecosystem. Microsoft also introduced Web IQ, a search engine defined as a “network intelligence layer for AI agents.”

China Macro & Policy

The State Council issued the “15th Five-Year Plan for Accelerating Agricultural and Rural Modernization,” outlining major projects including high-standard farmland construction and cold-chain logistics facilities. The plan promotes diversified food development and the “AI+” agriculture initiative, and identifies key policy measures to cultivate emerging and future industries in agriculture.

National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) Director Zheng Zhijie convened a meeting with state-owned enterprises to discuss deepening SOE reform, advancing the national unified market, promoting green and low-carbon development, and ensuring energy security. Zheng emphasized enhancing the technological innovation, industrial control, and security support functions of state-owned enterprises.

The Ministry of Finance recently allocated 99.9 billion yuan in childcare subsidies for 2026, a 10.6% increase from last year, to support local governments in providing subsidies to eligible infants. Total fiscal subsidies for the year are projected to reach approximately 110 billion yuan.

The People’s Bank of China reported that in May, standing lending facility (SLF) net injections amounted to 900 million yuan, pledged supplementary lending (PSL) net withdrawals totaled 154.5 billion yuan, and other structural monetary policy tools net injected 112.9 billion yuan; open market treasury purchases resulted in a net injection of 50 billion yuan.

China’s first online tax-refund-for-overseas-tourists store has officially launched in Beijing, alongside the simultaneous activation of centralized tax-refund points in the city’s hotel sector. Overseas visitors can now process tax refunds directly at hotels after online purchases, expanding the scope of tax-refund services.

China Equities Markets

A-share markets climbed modestly, with the SSE Index rising 0.43% to 4,075.10, the Shenzhen Component Index up 1.63%, and the ChiNext Index jumping 2.66%. The Wind All China Index gained 0.75%, with total market turnover reaching 2.81 trillion yuan. The AI computing hardware supply chain strengthened again, with CPO and optical fiber sectors surging. Components, AI PCs, PCBs, and MLCC concepts advanced. Coal stocks maintained strength, while film and theater, and short-form video game concepts declined.

The Hang Seng Index closed up 2.52% at 26,038.32, the Hang Seng Tech Index surged 4.72%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index rose 3%. Technology stocks rallied broadly, with Tencent Holdings soaring over 10%. New energy vehicles and non-ferrous metals led gains, while AI applications and coal stocks declined. Net inflows via the Southbound Stock Connect totaled 2.2 billion Hong Kong dollars, with Tencent Holdings experiencing 2.1 billion Hong Kong dollars in net outflows.

Fund managers led by Jin Zicai announced that to ensure stable fund operations and protect investor interests, subscription limits for multiple funds — including Caichong Growth Optimum, Caichong Value Momentum, Caichong Integrated Circuit Industry, and Caichong Quality Selection — have been lowered to 1,000 yuan.

The Shanghai Stock Exchange website shows that Yushu Technology’s STAR Market IPO status has changed to “submitted for registration.” Yushu Technology’s market director Huang Jiawei revealed that the company’s new-generation humanoid robot reference design, H2 Plus, developed jointly with NVIDIA, will launch in the second half of the year.

China Industry News

China has achieved a major breakthrough in optical communications. The world’s first S+C+L triple-band (short, conventional, and long wavelength) ultra-low-loss multi-core optical cable line has been officially commissioned in Qingdao, Shandong. The single-fiber capacity exceeds five times that of traditional fibers, fully meeting future demands for AI computing and terabit-per-second ultra-high-speed transmission.

Four ministries have launched the “Sword Net 2026” campaign to combat online copyright infringement, with a focus on AI-related copyright issues. The initiative targets violations such as “remixing,” “plagiarism,” and deepfake content in large model training datasets.

According to Centaline Property Research, 141,000 second-hand residential units were sold in 20 cities in May, up 19.3% year-over-year, with the growth rate expanding 6.3 percentage points from April. Beijing and Shanghai recorded 16,000 and 28,000 second-hand home sales respectively, up 12.1% and 30.9% year-over-year, marking the highest May levels in nearly five years. The average second-hand home price across 100 cities fell 0.32% month-over-month, while new home prices rose 0.16%.

Guangzhou’s Nansha District unveiled “Ten Measures” for real estate policy, launching an “old-home-for-new-home” initiative where proceeds from selling an old property can be directly applied toward a new purchase. A subsidy of 1% of the new home’s total price — capped at 20,000 yuan per unit — will be provided, and this can be stacked with municipal subsidies.

The China Passenger Car Association estimated that domestic new energy passenger vehicle wholesale sales reached 1.36 million units in May, up over 11% year-over-year and month-over-month. According to the China Automobile Dealers Association, the automobile consumption index stood at 81 in May, slightly higher than April, suggesting a continued steady upward trend in overall sales for June.

Fintech

Shanghai’s guidelines on deepening its global asset management center status encourage asset managers to leverage AI, blockchain, and big data technologies to accelerate intelligent investment research and advisory services. The city will support the establishment of AI application pilot bases for asset management.

The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission issued a circular requiring licensed institutions — particularly online brokers and virtual asset platforms — to strengthen cybersecurity measures to counter emerging threats driven by advanced AI models.

Corporate News & Updates

Sources indicate Tencent has completed prototype testing of its WeChat AI agent and will initiate compliance review procedures as early as this month. Users will be able to use the agent to trigger hundreds of mini-programs for daily tasks such as food ordering. The project has been designated the highest strategic priority. Tencent Cloud has reduced pricing for the DeepSeek-V4 series models by up to 97.5%.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang stated that agent-based AI will fundamentally reshape all AI applications and represent the most important computing transformation over the next decade. Huang also said Marvell Technology will become the next trillion-dollar company. NVIDIA announced full-scale mass production of its NVIDIA Spectrum-X Ethernet silicon photonics technology based on CPO.

Samsung Electronics unveiled its eighth-generation high-bandwidth memory chip, HBM5, and publicly introduced its HPB cooling solution for the first time. Chief Technology Officer Jae-Hyung Song confirmed that HPB technology has been validated on HBM4E chips and will be commercially deployed with HBM5.

Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger, in his Computex 2026 keynote, said agentic AI is reshaping computing demand, and future AI infrastructure will enter a heterogeneous era of co-development among CPUs, GPUs, ASICs, and custom chips. Demand for CPUs in data centers is expected to re-enter a growth cycle.

SK Group Chairman Choi Tae-won said the global shortage of memory chips may persist until 2030, and the company is increasing capital expenditures to bridge the supply-demand gap. SK Hynix plans to double its wafer capacity over the next five years.

ByteDance’s DouBao large model is expected to launch paid content in late June, with plans to integrate e-commerce functions in the third quarter to drive traffic to Douyin Mall. Longer-term operational strategies are not ruled out.

Anthropic announced the expansion of its Glasswing initiative to 150 new partner organizations across more than 15 countries. These institutions will use the Mythos tool to identify software vulnerabilities. New partners span energy, water, healthcare, telecommunications, and hardware industries.

OpenAI released a suite of new plugins for its Codex agent, covering public equity investment, banking, and sales use cases, and plans to introduce legal and corporate finance functions soon. OpenAI also intends to integrate Codex into ChatGPT.

Alibaba Cloud’s Qwen model launched Qwen3.7-Plus, a multimodal large model capable of understanding images and videos, performing deep reasoning, self-programming, tool invocation, validation, and autonomous iteration. It can complete complex, long-horizon tasks such as replicating smartphone apps and desktop software with a single command.

Global Macro & Policy

U.S. President Trump signed an executive order temporarily adjusting import tariffs on certain steel, aluminum, and copper products, effective June 8 through December 31, 2027. Import tariffs on harvesters and other agricultural machinery will be reduced from 25% to 15%, and the domestic content threshold for imported steel, aluminum, and copper will be lowered from 95% to 85%.

The United States plans to impose 25% punitive tariffs on a range of Brazilian imports, including electronic payment services, preferential tariff treatment, intellectual property protections, and ethanol market access. Exemptions include beef, coffee, rare earth minerals, aircraft and aircraft parts, crude oil, fertilizers, and numerous fruits and nuts.

The Canadian government has proposed to the U.S. and Mexico that the USMCA be renewed for 16 years to avoid triggering the agreement’s mandatory annual review process. Under USMCA provisions, the three nations are scheduled to conduct their first joint review in July. The agreement, signed in 2018 and effective since July 2020, is set to expire in 2036.

U.S. officials are reportedly discussing whether to deploy nuclear weapons in additional NATO European countries. The U.S. already deploys nuclear-capable bombers in Belgium, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Turkey, and the UK, and has signaled openness to additional deployments.

Hawkish voices within the Federal Reserve are intensifying. Cleveland Fed President Harker said while maintaining current rates remains appropriate, persistent inflation data may soon necessitate policy action — including potential further rate hikes — to counter elevated inflation risks.

Labor market resilience continues to bolster Fed hawkish expectations. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics data showed job openings rose to 7.62 million in April, the highest level in two years and well above the 6.87 million forecast. Layoffs fell to 1.692 million, significantly below expectations.

The European Union plans to impose strict eligibility criteria for cloud computing services in key public procurement projects, potentially excluding Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. The proposal aims to reduce EU dependence on U.S. tech products and promote domestic industry development.

Eurostat preliminary data showed euro area CPI rose 3.2% year-over-year in May, up from 3% in April, reinforcing market expectations of a modest ECB rate hike in June.

South Korea’s central bank is poised to raise rates. Statistics Korea reported that May CPI rose 3.1% year-over-year, the largest increase since March 2024. Core CPI increased 2.5%, the highest since February 2024.

The World Meteorological Organization released a report indicating most climate models predict this El Niño will develop into at least a moderate event and may intensify further. Seasonal forecasts suggest most regions will experience above-average temperatures in the coming months.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indices closed slightly higher: the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.45% to 51,307.79, the S&P 500 gained 0.13% to 7,609.78, and the Nasdaq Composite edged up 0.03% to 27,093.90 — all closing at record highs. Cisco rose over 5%, Caterpillar gained more than 5%, leading the Dow. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index declined 1.05%, with Microsoft down over 4% and Google down nearly 4%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index rose 1.83%, with Canadian Solar up over 12% and Hesai Group up over 8%. Traders closely monitored developments in U.S.-Iran relations and major tech stock performance.

European indices closed higher: Germany’s DAX rose 0.48% to 25,124.17, France’s CAC 40 gained 0.77% to 8,209.09, and the UK’s FTSE 100 climbed 0.33% to 10,373.51. Progress in ceasefire negotiations between Iran and the U.S. lifted risk appetite, but Brent crude breached $95 per barrel, maintaining upward pressure on inflation expectations.

Asia-Pacific markets closed mixed. South Korea’s KOSPI swung sharply — initially surging before plunging more than 3% — and ultimately closed up 0.15% at 8,801.49, setting a new record. South Korea’s total market capitalization surpassed India’s, becoming the world’s sixth-largest stock market. Japan’s Nikkei 225 fell 0.3% to 66,734.24. India’s SENSEX rose 0.52% to 74,649.84.

Andrew Left, founder of the renowned short-selling firm Citron Research, was convicted by a U.S. court of stock manipulation and may face decades in prison. The case could mark a landmark moment for short-sellers globally and reshape regulatory boundaries for short reports and market commentary.

Data from India’s National Securities Depository Limited showed that as of June 1, foreign institutional investors’ net investment in Indian equities totaled 7.3 trillion rupees — the lowest since 2016. Global fund ownership in Indian equities has declined from nearly 20% a decade ago to 15% today.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market traded in a narrow range, with yields on major government bonds moving slightly in different directions. Treasury futures rallied slightly at the close, with the 10-year contract up 0.06%. Liquidity conditions returned to ample levels as the PBOC conducted 200 million yuan in reverse repo operations, resulting in a net withdrawal of 248.8 billion yuan.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed: the 2-year yield rose 1.24 basis points to 4.039%, the 3-year yield rose 0.59 basis points to 4.085%, the 5-year yield held steady at 4.163%, the 10-year yield fell 1.18 basis points to 4.439%, and the 30-year yield declined 1.21 basis points to 4.957%.

Commodities

U.S. crude oil futures rose 1.33% to $93.39 per barrel; Brent crude gained 1.01% to $95.94 per barrel. U.S. API crude inventories fell sharply, exceeding market expectations, while geopolitical tensions in the Middle East — including Iran’s suspension of U.S.-Iran talks and its plan to block the Strait of Hormuz — heightened concerns over supply disruptions. The strait handles roughly 20% of global oil shipments, and global crude inventories remain at low levels, jointly supporting higher prices.

International precious metals futures generally rose: COMEX gold futures gained 0.29% to $4,519.20 per ounce, and COMEX silver futures rose 0.25% to $75.44 per ounce. Geopolitical tensions in the Middle East lifted safe-haven demand, while rising U.S. and European rate expectations weighed on market sentiment, creating a tug-of-war.

Most base metals on the LME rose: LME tin climbed 2.88% to $58,280 per ton, LME lead gained 1.52% to $2,037 per ton, LME zinc rose 1.48% to $3,631 per ton, LME aluminum advanced 1.20% to $3,760.50 per ton, LME copper increased 1.10% to $13,983.50 per ton, while LME nickel edged down 0.19% to $19,215 per ton.

The European Central Bank reported that as of the end of 2025, gold accounted for 27% of global official reserve assets, surpassing U.S. Treasuries, which fell to 22%. Gold has become the world’s largest official reserve asset.

Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC) revealed plans to construct a refined products pipeline to bypass the Strait of Hormuz. The company is also exploring a new east-west crude oil trunk line that would allow other Gulf producers to route exports through UAE territory, avoiding the strait.

FX & Currency Markets

Onshore renminbi closed at 6.7622 against the U.S. dollar at 16:30, up 28 basis points from the previous close, and settled at 6.7650 in the night session. The PBOC’s mid-rate was set at 6.8187, a 20-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At the close of New York trading, the U.S. Dollar Index rose 0.03% to 99.22. Non-U.S. currencies were mixed: the euro fell 0.01% to 1.1631, the pound rose 0.07% to 1.3464, the Australian dollar rose 0.27% to 0.7180, the dollar rose 0.16% against the yen to 159.9295, the dollar fell 0.01% against the Canadian dollar to 1.3839, the dollar rose 0.08% against the Swiss franc to 0.7873, and the offshore renminbi gained 23 basis points to 6.7629.

Japan’s authorities issued a strong signal after record-sized foreign exchange interventions proved ineffective. Finance Minister Kato said the government stands ready to take appropriate action on exchange rates if necessary.

Market Brief

The State Council issues the "15th Five-Year Plan for Accelerating Agricultural and Rural Modernization"

China achieves significant breakthrough in optical communication technology

Shanghai establishes a new landmark for the global asset management center: scale to reach RMB 5.5 trillion by 2030

Prospects for U.S.-Iran negotiations remain uncertain, while applications for transit permits through the Strait of Hormuz have opened

The United States lowers import tariffs on selected steel, aluminum, and copper products, and plans to impose a new 25% tariff on Brazil

Global stock markets rose Tuesday, with U.S. markets hitting new highs and South Korea’s market entering the world’s top six largest securities markets

Tiger Brokers: Suspends new position opening and additional positioning for existing investor accounts in all domestic Chinese instruments effective June 12

Microsoft releases seven new AI models to create an integrated “thinking + programming” agent ecosystem

Top Stories

Shanghai has unveiled 21 measures to deepen its construction as a global asset management center, aiming for total asset management scale of 5.4 trillion yuan by 2030, accounting for one-third of the national total. The city will support listings and mergers of technology firms with strong innovation profiles; accelerate the launch of LNG futures and options; prepare for power and computing power futures; promote the listing of Star 50 Index, Shenzhen 100 Index, ChiNext Index futures and options, and government bond options; enhance mechanisms such as “Shanghai-Hong Kong Stock Connect,” “China-Europe Connect,” and “Bond Connect”; and steadily expand institutional openness in the gold market.

Tiger Brokers announced that, to comply with regulatory requirements during the cross-border securities business consolidation period, it will adjust services for existing investor accounts in mainland China starting June 12. Measures include suspending new positions and additional purchases across all securities products, permitting only sell and close-out transactions; and halting inbound fund transfers while maintaining outbound transfer functionality. The company reported first-quarter revenue of $155 million, up 26.3% year-over-year, and operating profit of $48 million, up 17.5%.

According to Iranian sources, the system for applying for vessel transit permits through the Strait of Hormuz has been fully opened, allowing global shipowners and captains to submit applications. Upon approval, vessels will receive transit authorization. Iranian media reported that information exchanges between Iran and the U.S. have been suspended for several days, despite Western media and officials claiming ongoing dialogue. U.S. President Trump stated that talks with Iran continue. U.S. Secretary of State Rubio affirmed that negotiations are still underway but expressed uncertainty over a potential agreement.

Microsoft unveiled seven new AI models at the Build 2026 developer conference, covering full-stack capabilities in reasoning, coding, vision, speech, and multimodal functions. Its flagship reasoning model, MAI-Thinking-1, matched Anthropic’s Claude Sonnet 4.6 on coding benchmarks. The 5-billion-parameter coding model, MAI-Code-1-Flash, targets the GitHub ecosystem. Microsoft also introduced Web IQ, a search engine defined as a “network intelligence layer for AI agents.”

China Macro & Policy

The State Council issued the “15th Five-Year Plan for Accelerating Agricultural and Rural Modernization,” outlining major projects including high-standard farmland construction and cold-chain logistics facilities. The plan promotes diversified food development and the “AI+” agriculture initiative, and identifies key policy measures to cultivate emerging and future industries in agriculture.

National Development and Reform Commission (NDRC) Director Zheng Zhijie convened a meeting with state-owned enterprises to discuss deepening SOE reform, advancing the national unified market, promoting green and low-carbon development, and ensuring energy security. Zheng emphasized enhancing the technological innovation, industrial control, and security support functions of state-owned enterprises.

The Ministry of Finance recently allocated 99.9 billion yuan in childcare subsidies for 2026, a 10.6% increase from last year, to support local governments in providing subsidies to eligible infants. Total fiscal subsidies for the year are projected to reach approximately 110 billion yuan.

The People’s Bank of China reported that in May, standing lending facility (SLF) net injections amounted to 900 million yuan, pledged supplementary lending (PSL) net withdrawals totaled 154.5 billion yuan, and other structural monetary policy tools net injected 112.9 billion yuan; open market treasury purchases resulted in a net injection of 50 billion yuan.

China’s first online tax-refund-for-overseas-tourists store has officially launched in Beijing, alongside the simultaneous activation of centralized tax-refund points in the city’s hotel sector. Overseas visitors can now process tax refunds directly at hotels after online purchases, expanding the scope of tax-refund services.

China Equities Markets

A-share markets climbed modestly, with the SSE Index rising 0.43% to 4,075.10, the Shenzhen Component Index up 1.63%, and the ChiNext Index jumping 2.66%. The Wind All China Index gained 0.75%, with total market turnover reaching 2.81 trillion yuan. The AI computing hardware supply chain strengthened again, with CPO and optical fiber sectors surging. Components, AI PCs, PCBs, and MLCC concepts advanced. Coal stocks maintained strength, while film and theater, and short-form video game concepts declined.

The Hang Seng Index closed up 2.52% at 26,038.32, the Hang Seng Tech Index surged 4.72%, and the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index rose 3%. Technology stocks rallied broadly, with Tencent Holdings soaring over 10%. New energy vehicles and non-ferrous metals led gains, while AI applications and coal stocks declined. Net inflows via the Southbound Stock Connect totaled 2.2 billion Hong Kong dollars, with Tencent Holdings experiencing 2.1 billion Hong Kong dollars in net outflows.

Fund managers led by Jin Zicai announced that to ensure stable fund operations and protect investor interests, subscription limits for multiple funds — including Caichong Growth Optimum, Caichong Value Momentum, Caichong Integrated Circuit Industry, and Caichong Quality Selection — have been lowered to 1,000 yuan.

The Shanghai Stock Exchange website shows that Yushu Technology’s STAR Market IPO status has changed to “submitted for registration.” Yushu Technology’s market director Huang Jiawei revealed that the company’s new-generation humanoid robot reference design, H2 Plus, developed jointly with NVIDIA, will launch in the second half of the year.

China Industry News

China has achieved a major breakthrough in optical communications. The world’s first S+C+L triple-band (short, conventional, and long wavelength) ultra-low-loss multi-core optical cable line has been officially commissioned in Qingdao, Shandong. The single-fiber capacity exceeds five times that of traditional fibers, fully meeting future demands for AI computing and terabit-per-second ultra-high-speed transmission.

Four ministries have launched the “Sword Net 2026” campaign to combat online copyright infringement, with a focus on AI-related copyright issues. The initiative targets violations such as “remixing,” “plagiarism,” and deepfake content in large model training datasets.

According to Centaline Property Research, 141,000 second-hand residential units were sold in 20 cities in May, up 19.3% year-over-year, with the growth rate expanding 6.3 percentage points from April. Beijing and Shanghai recorded 16,000 and 28,000 second-hand home sales respectively, up 12.1% and 30.9% year-over-year, marking the highest May levels in nearly five years. The average second-hand home price across 100 cities fell 0.32% month-over-month, while new home prices rose 0.16%.

Guangzhou’s Nansha District unveiled “Ten Measures” for real estate policy, launching an “old-home-for-new-home” initiative where proceeds from selling an old property can be directly applied toward a new purchase. A subsidy of 1% of the new home’s total price — capped at 20,000 yuan per unit — will be provided, and this can be stacked with municipal subsidies.

The China Passenger Car Association estimated that domestic new energy passenger vehicle wholesale sales reached 1.36 million units in May, up over 11% year-over-year and month-over-month. According to the China Automobile Dealers Association, the automobile consumption index stood at 81 in May, slightly higher than April, suggesting a continued steady upward trend in overall sales for June.

Fintech

Shanghai’s guidelines on deepening its global asset management center status encourage asset managers to leverage AI, blockchain, and big data technologies to accelerate intelligent investment research and advisory services. The city will support the establishment of AI application pilot bases for asset management.

The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission issued a circular requiring licensed institutions — particularly online brokers and virtual asset platforms — to strengthen cybersecurity measures to counter emerging threats driven by advanced AI models.

Corporate News & Updates

Sources indicate Tencent has completed prototype testing of its WeChat AI agent and will initiate compliance review procedures as early as this month. Users will be able to use the agent to trigger hundreds of mini-programs for daily tasks such as food ordering. The project has been designated the highest strategic priority. Tencent Cloud has reduced pricing for the DeepSeek-V4 series models by up to 97.5%.

NVIDIA CEO Jensen Huang stated that agent-based AI will fundamentally reshape all AI applications and represent the most important computing transformation over the next decade. Huang also said Marvell Technology will become the next trillion-dollar company. NVIDIA announced full-scale mass production of its NVIDIA Spectrum-X Ethernet silicon photonics technology based on CPO.

Samsung Electronics unveiled its eighth-generation high-bandwidth memory chip, HBM5, and publicly introduced its HPB cooling solution for the first time. Chief Technology Officer Jae-Hyung Song confirmed that HPB technology has been validated on HBM4E chips and will be commercially deployed with HBM5.

Intel CEO Pat Gelsinger, in his Computex 2026 keynote, said agentic AI is reshaping computing demand, and future AI infrastructure will enter a heterogeneous era of co-development among CPUs, GPUs, ASICs, and custom chips. Demand for CPUs in data centers is expected to re-enter a growth cycle.

SK Group Chairman Choi Tae-won said the global shortage of memory chips may persist until 2030, and the company is increasing capital expenditures to bridge the supply-demand gap. SK Hynix plans to double its wafer capacity over the next five years.

ByteDance’s DouBao large model is expected to launch paid content in late June, with plans to integrate e-commerce functions in the third quarter to drive traffic to Douyin Mall. Longer-term operational strategies are not ruled out.

Anthropic announced the expansion of its Glasswing initiative to 150 new partner organizations across more than 15 countries. These institutions will use the Mythos tool to identify software vulnerabilities. New partners span energy, water, healthcare, telecommunications, and hardware industries.

OpenAI released a suite of new plugins for its Codex agent, covering public equity investment, banking, and sales use cases, and plans to introduce legal and corporate finance functions soon. OpenAI also intends to integrate Codex into ChatGPT.

Alibaba Cloud’s Qwen model launched Qwen3.7-Plus, a multimodal large model capable of understanding images and videos, performing deep reasoning, self-programming, tool invocation, validation, and autonomous iteration. It can complete complex, long-horizon tasks such as replicating smartphone apps and desktop software with a single command.

Global Macro & Policy

U.S. President Trump signed an executive order temporarily adjusting import tariffs on certain steel, aluminum, and copper products, effective June 8 through December 31, 2027. Import tariffs on harvesters and other agricultural machinery will be reduced from 25% to 15%, and the domestic content threshold for imported steel, aluminum, and copper will be lowered from 95% to 85%.

The United States plans to impose 25% punitive tariffs on a range of Brazilian imports, including electronic payment services, preferential tariff treatment, intellectual property protections, and ethanol market access. Exemptions include beef, coffee, rare earth minerals, aircraft and aircraft parts, crude oil, fertilizers, and numerous fruits and nuts.

The Canadian government has proposed to the U.S. and Mexico that the USMCA be renewed for 16 years to avoid triggering the agreement’s mandatory annual review process. Under USMCA provisions, the three nations are scheduled to conduct their first joint review in July. The agreement, signed in 2018 and effective since July 2020, is set to expire in 2036.

U.S. officials are reportedly discussing whether to deploy nuclear weapons in additional NATO European countries. The U.S. already deploys nuclear-capable bombers in Belgium, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, Turkey, and the UK, and has signaled openness to additional deployments.

Hawkish voices within the Federal Reserve are intensifying. Cleveland Fed President Harker said while maintaining current rates remains appropriate, persistent inflation data may soon necessitate policy action — including potential further rate hikes — to counter elevated inflation risks.

Labor market resilience continues to bolster Fed hawkish expectations. U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics data showed job openings rose to 7.62 million in April, the highest level in two years and well above the 6.87 million forecast. Layoffs fell to 1.692 million, significantly below expectations.

The European Union plans to impose strict eligibility criteria for cloud computing services in key public procurement projects, potentially excluding Amazon, Microsoft, and Google. The proposal aims to reduce EU dependence on U.S. tech products and promote domestic industry development.

Eurostat preliminary data showed euro area CPI rose 3.2% year-over-year in May, up from 3% in April, reinforcing market expectations of a modest ECB rate hike in June.

South Korea’s central bank is poised to raise rates. Statistics Korea reported that May CPI rose 3.1% year-over-year, the largest increase since March 2024. Core CPI increased 2.5%, the highest since February 2024.

The World Meteorological Organization released a report indicating most climate models predict this El Niño will develop into at least a moderate event and may intensify further. Seasonal forecasts suggest most regions will experience above-average temperatures in the coming months.

Global Equity Markets

U.S. major indices closed slightly higher: the Dow Jones Industrial Average rose 0.45% to 51,307.79, the S&P 500 gained 0.13% to 7,609.78, and the Nasdaq Composite edged up 0.03% to 27,093.90 — all closing at record highs. Cisco rose over 5%, Caterpillar gained more than 5%, leading the Dow. The Wind US Magnificent Seven Index declined 1.05%, with Microsoft down over 4% and Google down nearly 4%. The Nasdaq China Golden Dragon Index rose 1.83%, with Canadian Solar up over 12% and Hesai Group up over 8%. Traders closely monitored developments in U.S.-Iran relations and major tech stock performance.

European indices closed higher: Germany’s DAX rose 0.48% to 25,124.17, France’s CAC 40 gained 0.77% to 8,209.09, and the UK’s FTSE 100 climbed 0.33% to 10,373.51. Progress in ceasefire negotiations between Iran and the U.S. lifted risk appetite, but Brent crude breached $95 per barrel, maintaining upward pressure on inflation expectations.

Asia-Pacific markets closed mixed. South Korea’s KOSPI swung sharply — initially surging before plunging more than 3% — and ultimately closed up 0.15% at 8,801.49, setting a new record. South Korea’s total market capitalization surpassed India’s, becoming the world’s sixth-largest stock market. Japan’s Nikkei 225 fell 0.3% to 66,734.24. India’s SENSEX rose 0.52% to 74,649.84.

Andrew Left, founder of the renowned short-selling firm Citron Research, was convicted by a U.S. court of stock manipulation and may face decades in prison. The case could mark a landmark moment for short-sellers globally and reshape regulatory boundaries for short reports and market commentary.

Data from India’s National Securities Depository Limited showed that as of June 1, foreign institutional investors’ net investment in Indian equities totaled 7.3 trillion rupees — the lowest since 2016. Global fund ownership in Indian equities has declined from nearly 20% a decade ago to 15% today.

Bonds

China’s interbank bond market traded in a narrow range, with yields on major government bonds moving slightly in different directions. Treasury futures rallied slightly at the close, with the 10-year contract up 0.06%. Liquidity conditions returned to ample levels as the PBOC conducted 200 million yuan in reverse repo operations, resulting in a net withdrawal of 248.8 billion yuan.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed: the 2-year yield rose 1.24 basis points to 4.039%, the 3-year yield rose 0.59 basis points to 4.085%, the 5-year yield held steady at 4.163%, the 10-year yield fell 1.18 basis points to 4.439%, and the 30-year yield declined 1.21 basis points to 4.957%.

Commodities

U.S. crude oil futures rose 1.33% to $93.39 per barrel; Brent crude gained 1.01% to $95.94 per barrel. U.S. API crude inventories fell sharply, exceeding market expectations, while geopolitical tensions in the Middle East — including Iran’s suspension of U.S.-Iran talks and its plan to block the Strait of Hormuz — heightened concerns over supply disruptions. The strait handles roughly 20% of global oil shipments, and global crude inventories remain at low levels, jointly supporting higher prices.

International precious metals futures generally rose: COMEX gold futures gained 0.29% to $4,519.20 per ounce, and COMEX silver futures rose 0.25% to $75.44 per ounce. Geopolitical tensions in the Middle East lifted safe-haven demand, while rising U.S. and European rate expectations weighed on market sentiment, creating a tug-of-war.

Most base metals on the LME rose: LME tin climbed 2.88% to $58,280 per ton, LME lead gained 1.52% to $2,037 per ton, LME zinc rose 1.48% to $3,631 per ton, LME aluminum advanced 1.20% to $3,760.50 per ton, LME copper increased 1.10% to $13,983.50 per ton, while LME nickel edged down 0.19% to $19,215 per ton.

The European Central Bank reported that as of the end of 2025, gold accounted for 27% of global official reserve assets, surpassing U.S. Treasuries, which fell to 22%. Gold has become the world’s largest official reserve asset.

Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC) revealed plans to construct a refined products pipeline to bypass the Strait of Hormuz. The company is also exploring a new east-west crude oil trunk line that would allow other Gulf producers to route exports through UAE territory, avoiding the strait.

FX & Currency Markets

Onshore renminbi closed at 6.7622 against the U.S. dollar at 16:30, up 28 basis points from the previous close, and settled at 6.7650 in the night session. The PBOC’s mid-rate was set at 6.8187, a 20-basis-point depreciation from the prior day.

At the close of New York trading, the U.S. Dollar Index rose 0.03% to 99.22. Non-U.S. currencies were mixed: the euro fell 0.01% to 1.1631, the pound rose 0.07% to 1.3464, the Australian dollar rose 0.27% to 0.7180, the dollar rose 0.16% against the yen to 159.9295, the dollar fell 0.01% against the Canadian dollar to 1.3839, the dollar rose 0.08% against the Swiss franc to 0.7873, and the offshore renminbi gained 23 basis points to 6.7629.

Japan’s authorities issued a strong signal after record-sized foreign exchange interventions proved ineffective. Finance Minister Kato said the government stands ready to take appropriate action on exchange rates if necessary.

Photonics names back 

$AAOI $LITE led yesterday and today we saw other names like $GLW $COHR have strong days as well 

Like the look of some other names into tomorrow as well in this basket like $VIAV https://t.co/BuxbmFBvso

$lwlg Luminary 2회차 발간함

내용은 뭐 새로운 건 없음. 

PDK 1.1, 마이클 베스트랑 IP 자문계약한거 리마인드 정도..

단기 행사 일정으로는, 

1. Photonics North
- CTO 아레프 초우더리가 6월 4일 업계 기조 강연 진행

2. IEEE 광 인터커넥트 및 패키징(OIP) 컨퍼런스(6월 15~17일)
- Director of Reliability인 마노지 만자레가 6월 16일 발표 예정

그리고, Semiconductor Leadership Podcast에도 출연한다고 함. 

반도체 업계가 실리콘 포토닉스가 400G 이상의 광 네트워킹과 차세대 AI 연결을 어떻게 구현할 수 있는지, 그리고 더 낮은 전력 소비와 더 높은 집적도로 더 높은 대역폭을 제공하는 데 필요한 요소는 무엇인지에 점점 더 집중하고 있는 것과 관련하여, 

$lwlg SVP of Sales and Marketing인 로버트 블룸이 ISIG CEO 살라 나스리가 진행하는 반도체 리더십 팟캐스트 에 게스트로 출연하여 이 주제들을 심도 있게 다룰 예정. 

뭔가 좀 나와야 한다 $lwlg...

Original Air Date:  02.JUN.2026
8:00 PM EST

#1202 //  THE AI RACE - LIVE
In this first episode of The AI Race, we cut through the noise to deliver a clear-eyed survey of where artificial intelligence actually stands in 2026. We examine the real capabilities and hard limitations of current systems, the massive infrastructure and energy demands driving the buildout, and the technical paths — including photonics — that could help ease those pressures. We also explore the global competition shaping this technology, how narratives around AI and data centers are being shaped and amplified, and the serious questions of economic impact, power concentration, and governance that will determine who benefits. Along the way, we separate legitimate concerns from exaggerated fears while giving voice to the real experiences many had during recent years of information control. We close with late-breaking developments across geopolitics and global institutions, and a grounded look at what’s truly at stake as this race accelerates.
#QBits

text:
the alpha corner is gold if you ask me 👀 (i'm obviously biased)

I built the daily brief because I was tired of reading the same AI take 40 times a day and still not knowing where the buildout was actually tightening.

So now every morning it goes through the whole bottleneck book, memory, packaging, photonics, power, grid, cooling, and flags what's coiled before it's obvious. great way to synthetise everything and find where the pressure is.

have a look for yourself!

text:
The AI infrastructure buildout is barely in its first innings.

Everyone on here is trying to figure out where in the stack they want to be.

Power. Compute. HPC DCs. Photonics. Memory. Networking. Cooling. Models/Apps.

Some of those layers I understand well. Some I understand just enough to be dangerous.

That is why I keep coming back to the most physical parts of the stack.

Land. Power. Buildings. Capacity. Hyperscalers and AI labs who need more compute than the world can currently provide.

That is $IREN to me.

The market spent years treating it like its old business, and probably still does to some extent until AI revenue really shows up.

I think the real story is power-backed infrastructure as compute becomes one of the scarcest resources on earth.

$DGXX is earlier, smaller and more execution heavy, but that is part of the appeal to me.

It feels closer to an early mini $NBIS style setup if they execute.

Same bet: compute demand is a 2 to 3 decade buildout.

The sheer deficit in compute gives me sleep at night comfort. That is why I have sized these names accordingly.

And dare I say it, I think this sector can 100x over the next few decades.

I think we are still early in figuring out who owns the real choke points.

People like @jiahanjimliu, @Agrippa_Inv and @FransBakker9812 are deeper in parts of this stack, but this is how I’m framing it.

user_name: アスナロDrive｜30代共働きのオルカン+α投資@宇宙関連勉強中
text:
■「コンポーネント企業」のニッチな独占構造
＜高い参入障壁と認証の壁＞
宇宙空間は、激しい温度変化、放射線、真空状態、そして打ち上げ時の凄まじい振動に耐えなければなりません。
一度「宇宙での稼働実績（Flight Heritage）」を得たコンポーネントは、競合が容易にリプレイスできないため、先行者が市場を独占する構造です。

＜主要プレイヤー＞
Mynaric（$MYV / ドイツ・米国）:
LEO衛星向け光通信ターミナルのパイオニア。
SpaceX、DARPA、Northrop Grummanなどに製品を供給するセクターの代表格。

CACI International（$CACI）:
SA Photonicsを買収し、米政府・防衛向けの超高信頼性レーザー通信コンポーネントで圧倒的なシェア。

国内の動向:
ソニーグループが「ソニー宇宙光通信（SSC）」を設立して民間LEO方向への量産化を狙っているほか、三菱電機やNECなどの重電大手が防衛省向けに強みを持っています。

The future is optical.
Data moving through LIGHT.

And $SIVE sits right in this theme with DFB lasers, optical interconnects, 1.6T transceiver exposure, Jabil collaboration, Ayar Labs partnership and CPO/NPO optionality.

NVIDIA pushing into photonics could wake up the entire market.

$SIVE the setup is getting very, very serious. $NVDA

text:
Interesting article from Swedish media..

Nvidia is investing $6.5 billion into photonics.

Why? Data centers consume too much power. The fix: move data with light instead of electricity.

This is exactly the market $SIVE operates in.

Nvidia isn’t building this alone, it needs component suppliers. Sivers is one of the few publicly listed InP photonics companies in the world.

• Coherent — $2B + purchase commitments
• Lumentum — $2B
• Marvell — $2B (silicon photonics)
• Corning — $500M
• Ayar Labs — $500M Series 

Wouldn’t be suprised seeing $NVDA funding $SIVE too at some point for CPO build-out

text:
Those 2 things are not mutually exclusive.  I would say based on my analysis that it a solid company, though much of the move to photonics and power semis will take a long time, that is a small % of rhe mix rn.  You have mostly auto and industrial exposure.  

The point I want to make though is a bigger one.  You have to look at the stock separate from the company because there is one other key variable, the price you pay.  A Ferarri is a great car isnt it?  But would you pay $10 million for on?  Probably no, even id you could afford it.   

You can make money buying mediocre companies and prices that bake in them being horrible companies. You can make money buying great companies when they are priced reasonably relative to their future growth prospects.  

Entry point is the key.  If you massively overpay for an asset, be it a middling one or a trophy asset, it may take a decade to recover from that mistake.

text:
$SIVE is one of the more overlooked semiconductor plays in Europe.

The company develops high-performance RF and photonics chips used in:
• AI datacenters
• Optical networking
• 5G infrastructure
• Satellite communications
Revenue grew 33% in 2025, while management is exploring a potential U.S. dual listing to increase visibility. 

With growing exposure to AI and data infrastructure, $SIVE is a stock worth keeping on the radar.

It pumped nicely last few months, but with only 2 bil. mc, there is still much potential in it.

#SIVE #Semiconductors #AI #Stocks
